sentences.text: Yes.
To go on the Kindertransport.
I had three or four days.
Yeah.
At that time things were so urgent, that there was hardly any discussion.
I can tell you the last thing my father said to me.
I can tell you that in a minute, but first I want you to understand what the children's transports were.

The British government had said they would take 10,000 children, selected by some German Jewish organization, and the government would be the sponsor, as it were, would look after them until they could be on their own.
As far as, to my knowledge, the only government action taken by a civilized-- ugh, civilized-- country, and for [INAUDIBLE] almost like winning the Irish sweepstakes, I was chosen for this.
Most of the people chosen were the children of Jews who'd come from Poland, whom the Germans had taken, and tried to push into Poland.
The Poles had tried to hold them, and they were lying in the [INAUDIBLE] area, being filed on by both sides.
There were children [? left on that. ?] Some of them came on these children's transports.
But my suspicion is that I was chosen because I was closer-- becoming closer, and closer to 16.
And the Nazis, in a very organized German fashion, tended to leave people under 16 alone, and over 16 put them in the concentration, and slave labor camps.
Anyway, I was chosen.
So I came home one day from school, I forget, a Wednesday or-- I'm not quite sure.
Yes.
And my aunt said, mother had just called.
I was to go home immediately, I was leaving for England on Tuesday.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I did.
I know we went around a little bit.
My mother bought me a suit and bought me all kinds of things.
The point was I could take with me anything I could carry.
So I had a rucksack and a-- a case that I could handle.
That was it.
No gold, no silver, no nothing.
We went to Leipzig, this was also fairly close to Chemnitz--
Yes.
Well, somebody did, yes.
Not really, just, you're going to England, we're delighted, we hope, and so on, so on.
I have sometimes tried to imagine, you know, what it meant to parents to send their oldest son, 16, to England.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That doesn't do me any good at all.
I better not think along those lines.
To me, it was, you know, an enormous whirlwind, you see.
We went, the night before the train was to go, to Leipzig, which is close to Chemnitz, also, where we stayed the night with my cousin.

And the next day we went to the station, and it turned out that the two front carts were full of these children's transport kids, and one compartment, or two compartments, [INAUDIBLE] of the next car, was children's transports.
And I was in one of these compartments.
And that trip is also something that will stay with me.
I can tell you a number of things.
He was in Germany.
He went home with them again.
And in order to not feel quite so lonely, you know, with my bed empty, he put our teddy bear-- although we were much too old to play with teddy bears, this was a historical thing-- into my bed.
Nothing.
Nothing at all.
Do you remember the last night with your parents?
Not really.
Not really.
That's right.
I remember being on the platform with my parents and my brother, and my father took me aside, asked, did I know all about [INAUDIBLE]?
I said, yes, I thought so, mother had told us.
To this day I'm not quite sure what he would have done, or what he could have done if I had said, no.
But he actually was not terribly good with children.
He loved them.
He respected them tremendously.
But in terms of the day-by-day things, he didn't do very much.
And they he said something to me, which I've never forgotten.
He says to me, Walter, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
In English, Water, whatever you do be circumspect, and don't forget where you come from.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] OK.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] train, waved goodbye, the last time I saw my parents.
Not really.
I mean, there obviously was some.
She was a really loving woman.
And-- I kind of, in a way you might say, feel her skin, the skin of her hands, now, in a way.
So it must have been quite something.
But the only, you might call, halfway serious thing I remember, is my father's remark.
Not at all, no.
I-- I had no sense not to, either.
I got on this train and apparently had really no sense, period.
For example, my father had done something quite risky, something he wouldn't ordinarily do.
He took off his watch, a gold, International Company Schaffhausen, a gold Swiss watch, which he'd worn for, I think longer that I was born, just after I was born.
My mother brought to him once for-- you know, big case made off-- and he gave-- and I gave him my steel watch, and I see me put on his gold watch.
The idea was if the Nazis had seen it, say, I'm so sorry.
I forgot about this, they were taking and presumably left me alone.
I don't know.
I had this watch for a long while.
I gave it to my son when he reached the age that I was at that time, close to 16, and was stolen from him, he lost it.
I was this, maybe-- this sort of gives you the idea of the kind of things you found, I mean, on the train.
There was a girl sitting with us, real younger than I, I think, maybe, say, 13, I don't know how old.
And she was obviously an extremely confused young woman.

I asked a few questions, turned out she had been brought up by a loving Catholic couple-- Catholic-- and had been told, I think a couple of days before, she was adopted, she was really Jewish, and she better get out.
No wonder she was a bit whoo.
And she was wearing a little gold cross, OK?
And some idiot told her, you know, spoke about how dangerous it was to have any gold and silver.
She went into absolute panic, wanted to throw it out of the window.
I said, give it to me.
And she did, and I put it down the seat of the thing-- all right-- of the seat.
Well then suddenly, we hadn't seen a Nazi guard or heard of one.
There was a bell sound on the-- on the track.
And a moment later, a kid from the front coaches came into our apartment and said, we have crossed into Holland.
We are in Holland.
I think most of us didn't believe it, we were so, so amazed.
But then it occurred to me, I was in Holland, no Nazi would get after me.

And I had a feeling then, which also have never forgotten, and it's strong with me today-- I knew that I would never, ever, live again under the kind of circumstances, the totalitarianism, and so on, that I was leaving.
It was very strong.
Very shortly, we rolled into a Dutch station.
There were all kinds of ladies there who fed us candy, and cookies, and drinks, and so on, so on.
We were there.
I just want to go back because I'm-- I'm not really clear on one thing.
What-- tell me about the selection process.
Would you know anything about the selection process for the Kinder--
I have no idea.
I do know that my father felt a little bit bad about my being chosen because he had gone to Berlin, to these people, and in a sense, complained that nobody from our area had ever been chosen.
So the next time-- round, I was chosen, which wasn't his intention, or his-- and he-- he just didn't know that was because of his-- him anyway, but I know he felt a bit bad about that.
No.
You see, look-- it's-- it's very difficult to explain this.
I was 16.
I was a-- I was an immature 16.
I had been-- I was bright, right, I mean, I could tell you all about Latin, and Aristotle, and so and so on.

But I was an immature youngster, who had been, as I told you many times, protected very carefully from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the-- you know, from the unpleasant events of the day-- had been a very spoiled kid.
And this-- these three, or four days, I was going to say it was a nightmare, but it wasn't a nightmare.
It was a roller coaster ride of a most enormous speed, and I simply-- [SIGHS] you know, I'll tell you in a minute what happened in England, if you want to know.
You see?
But first of all, let me make a point, from what I just said, you probably noticed that I'm not a survivor as to what they usually mean by survivors.
Usually they mean survivors of the camps.
I was fortunate enough to never get near the camps.
So I'm a survivor with a smaller S.
I'm not really, technically, a survivor.
We got to-- to Hoek van Holland, got onto a small ship, crossing the channel.
I got fairly seasick, but helped myself by simply sitting on the top deck-- it wasn't too bad.
And pretty soon we arrived in England.
Fine.
Got-- got onto a bus, got into a quite luxurious camp.
Actually, even today, I think, they still exist, the [INAUDIBLE] holiday camps.
And this was one of these holiday camps.
It was February, it was winter.
So, you know, we-- we-- the government, or something, had rented it, or taken over.
It was really quite pleasant, in many ways-- long huts with single rooms, and so on, so on.
And terrible mud in anything else.
And very soon after, I talked to the kids there.
You see, I'd been there for three weeks, four weeks.
I can't be here for three, or four weeks, I have to go to work to get my people out.
What is this ?] three, or four weeks?
Let me put it this way, I'm, to this day, rather pleased that there was no American psychologists, like me, or social worker, or anything like that around.
Because if there had been, I would have quite clearly had been diagnosed with a major nervous breakdown.
I suppose what happened was, I tried to grow up enormously quickly.
What I did do was, I hung around the center of the camp, and anybody who would listen to me, you know, who would talk to me, I would tell of how urgent it was to get my parents out and my brother.
Right?

And indeed, as it happened-- that's a rather long, and complex story-- but there-- as it happened, a woman very-- you know, working in the refugee departments, in the-- in the offices, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] interested himself-- herself, and somehow, by hook or by crook, and it really was being the crook, [? sought ?] that-- that my brother came.
So he came-- I mean, I arrived in England, February the 8th.
He came as in May 5.
February, March, April-- wait, two or three months later, he did come.
Yes, it's a long story.
The lady, this-- this woman, this English woman, Ms.
Macobi, OK, who was a secretary in the refugee organizations, whom I hated with bitterness.
Macobi.
Macobi, Ms.
Macobi said he'd [INAUDIBLE] my brother.
And she told me in a little while that she's found a sponsor.
And I wanted to know who that sponsor was, and thank him, meet him, and so on, so on, and she waved me off.
Turns out the sponsor was a nice little Jew, who'd signed anything they asked him to.
He couldn't have sponsored my brother anymore than he-- than he could have sponsored himself.
You see?
And the refugee committee found these out, 24 hours before my brother was due to sail on the I believe it was, in a very big ship.
So they immediately, you know, all the phones to Germany-- hold-- keep this guy out, I mean, he's not a sponsor.
He's, you know, he's nothing.
The lady who was supposed to make the phone call had a son who had the influenza, or something, that day, she didn't come in, and that's how my brother got to England.
Wow.
Oh, yeah.
And we had quite a bit to do to, you know, to set him up, and to help him, and get him to school, and so on, so on.
Well, we had both learned English in school.
The trouble with that was that we spoke English grandly-- I got the ones, and As, and so on, but the English didn't.
To be serious about it, English is very easy to learn, the grammar and everything, but it's very, very difficult to speak because it's all idioms, and usages, and so on and so on.
So the answer to the question is, well, we-- we knew some words, but we didn't really speak English.
If you want me to, I'll tell you some stories about these idioms.
Nothing, really.
I mean, the parents were as they always were.
There wasn't even much going on in those two-- few months that, you know, he wanted to tell me about.
And also, of course, he was a very unhappy kid, to start with.
He was put through a very pleasant school, where they tried to look after him, but, I mean, really, he was alone there.
I mean, it was pretty damn awful.
In the end, I managed to get him closer to where I was, and then I moved to where he was, and that's another very long story, [INAUDIBLE] one, but in the end we got together.
Well, we can look up the date.
I-- I-- I think that's my defense.
I don't remember dates.
I don't know when my father died, my mother-- I just don't remember these things.
But relatively soon after we left, after Kristallnacht, my father was prohibited any activity.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I managed, somehow-- that's another very long, and-- and very involved story, to get the English visa for my parents.
My parents kept [? bugging me about ?] buying this, buying that-- setting up what's called lifts in those days.
Enormous creates, you see, in which-- [INAUDIBLE] getting them ready.
You see, I was thinking why are you getting ready lifts for?
Come here already.
But, as I told you before, I don't think my father really wanted to come.

Not only because of his German philosophy, but also because he knew perfectly well that a German lawyer had very little chance of making a living, let alone for his children, you know, in countries other than Germany.
So what he did was, he hesitated.
You see?
I can show you the telegram.
A few days before war broke out, I wired them.
I said, Paul was very ill-- I hoped they would realize, Paul was standing for politics, or something, or [INAUDIBLE]-- better you come immediately.
And my father wired back to the effect that I was to keep an eye open and so on, so on.
He hoped he wasn't quite that bad, but if necessary, he'd come alone.
Like hell, he'd come alone.
I mean, I knew my father wouldn't come alone.
Well, after that, you see, pretty soon, then, war broke out, and of course, all visas were cancelled.
The English visas were no good.
We got in this situation, where the Dutch wouldn't let you in [? as ?] the British said they would.
And the British would say, you can come if you went to Holland, and this catch-22 killed more people than you can shake a stick at.
Nevertheless-- that's another very long story, [INAUDIBLE] Germany.
Nevertheless, I somehow managed to get [? another ?] Secretary of State to say he would sign admission, if the Refugee Committee would ask for it.

I didn't think for a moment that the Refugee Committee could mess this one up, but, of course, a week later, I got a letter-- phone call from the man, who had helped me, marvelous chap, that the-- the answer was, no.
I know now that they tried everything they possibly could to get out.
I have the passport, if you want to see it, has visa for Haiti, for Panama, for Shanghai, and if they wanted to other places-- England, of course, as I just mentioned.
They didn't make it.
And we wrote by Red Cross letters.
OK?
I was afraid to write too much to my mother or father, to make-- make the Germans, see, they got sons in England.
But we wrote a few and they wrote a few.
And-- so we found out the father was, had been dead.
And the next thing we knew, or one of the next things we knew, was this famous death certificate from the [INAUDIBLE], which is the registry of Dachau.
Well, my uncle, who was in London, knew about it.
And he didn't dare tell us, until somebody, a friend of my father, said he would, if he didn't.
So we found out that Father was gone.

And then a year, or two later-- you see, now we knew that things were strange-- they left [INAUDIBLE], our old address, the apartment, they had gone to somewhere else, which we imagine was a Jewish ghetto-type thing-- a house.
And then after Father's death, Mother went to Berlin to a certain place.

And then she, very carefully, wrote a letter to the lady-- this marvelous lady, who looked after my brother and me, most beautiful person-- to say that she was moving her-- her-- her place of-- of living-- of-- living place, which we knew exactly what it meant.
The lady saw the Red Cross letter and very happily gave it to my brother.
So he got full in the face to see that his mother was being sent East.
And for years we didn't know what had happened to her, but relatively recently-- a book of names, and so on, that she was shot in Riga.
Yes, by-- by one of the transports, the transports East.
An earlier booklet tells us that she went on such and such transport, East, East Auschwitz.
But apparently didn't go to Auschwitz, went to Riga, apparently.
When-- when the war was over-- I'm staying with your parents-- when-- when the war was over--
Forgive me, when I was staying with my parents?
Did you say when I was staying with my parents?
I'm sorry.
When the war was over, in 1945-- Yes.
Well, yes, of course.
We knew what had happened to my father.
You see, that was the registry, the [INAUDIBLE].
My mother had said for years, we didn't know.
Did I ever go back to Germany?
No.
I have-- I wished him well, but, unlike my brother, he would like to go back to Chemnitz.
But I have no intention of going back to Germany.
When I flew to Israel, I felt a little, how should I put it, a little pressure on my chest when we flew over the damn place.
But no, I didn't go back.
Yeah.
Well, yes, of course.
I mean, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The British are a strange people.
We, my brother and I, love England very, very much.
Whenever we can, we go there.
And I could tell you fantastic stories about individuals, who went far out of their way to help us, be good to us.
We-- we-- you know, I got a very good position from the refugee camp, from a group of local people who collected money for this purpose.
They didn't even bother to tell me they were a Christian-- a church group.
Didn't even bother to tell us, let alone trying to convert us, or something.
I could tell you that at great deal.

But I can also tell you that, particularly British [? in ?] authority, you know, [? the priority-- ?] not the police officer on the beat, but the police inspector, or the undersecretary of state, or some [INAUDIBLE], you know, they can be about as, I don't know, stiff, and-- and-- and inhuman as you can get them-- now, wait a minute, this was going to be an answer to your question-- oh, yes, what happened in England?

My brother was too young, but anybody my age, 16 and old, I've forgotten which-- 17, whatever-- we went before tribunals, which were going to judge our loyalty, our-- you know, how far we could be trusted.
And we could get three classifications, either you couldn't very well be trusted, and then you got put in an internment camp immediately.
Or you could, but very restricted.
Or you are really trustworthy, and you can go where you'd like and do what you like.
Well, I got number one, the top one, the best one.
They wanted to give me number two, but I explained to them why I wanted number one, so they gave it to me.
But after the Fall of France, [INAUDIBLE]--
The Fall of France.
Obviously there was a fair amount of panic and pressure in England.
And we know now that some idiot must have given the-- the order, intern the lot.
You couldn't intern the lot-- they didn't have enough room for them.
But in that first thing, I got caught.
I was interned.
I packed a [INAUDIBLE] bag ready for this, against this.
We were interned.
And my internment was in a way rather funny.
I was what the newspapers later wrote it up as a temple of horror, and of-- of nastiness.
The German-- I mean-- the British concentration camp.
It was nothing of the sort.
I mean, it was-- didn't like it, of course.
And then they tried terribly hard to release the people.
There was trouble with that.
[INAUDIBLE] for having been taking in, your livelihood, that you had, maybe, built up in a year or two, of course, was gone.
You see?
But they did say that anyone under 18 should be released.
So I was released.
I then wrote a few letters.
I don't know if they had anything to do with it.
The British would not permit us into the Army because-- I suppose that was very sensible.
They did not want to obligate themselves to make our 100,000 or whatever British citizens.
In the end, they did.
In the end-- [INAUDIBLE].
But they didn't want to be obligated.
So I wrote quite a few letters along those lines.

And I do not know to this date whether I had anything to do with it, or not, but I was one of the first aliens to be permitted to join the Home Guard, which was the-- do you know what the Home Guard was?
Why don't you tell me about it.

Well, the-- the-- it was-- people who were there, either too old or too young, having too much trouble-- not too much trouble-- having [? war work ?] or something, who wanted to serve, and they would back up the regular army.
So we were trained somewhat militarily, and played around with guarding, and stuff like that.
And I was that, anyway.
OK.
